content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 19, 1976

Mr. F. E. Jarrett, Supt.
Burney County Water District
Post Office Box 247
Burney, California 96013

Dear Mr. Jarrett:

Thank you for your letter of September 9, 1976, transmitting
a resolution adopted by the Board of Directors of Burney County
Water District. In essence, the resolution asks the Legislature
to amend the Political Reform Act of 1974 to exempt water district
boards of directors from the financial disclosure provisions of
Government Code §87100 et seq.

No public official at any level of state
or local government shall make, participate
in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a govern-
mental decision in which he knows or has
reason to know he has a financial interest.

It is understandable that non-salaried water district directors
believe it is an imposition to be compelled to disclose certain pri-
vate financial interests. However, under existing law, water dis-
tricts are governmental agencies; members of boards of directors as
well as certain administrative personnel within the districts "make
or participate in making governmental decisions" within the meaning
of the Political Reform Act; and many of their decisions affect pri-
vate financial interests. Under these circumstances, the Act requires
that water districts adopt Conflict of Interest Codes applicable to
officials and employees who are capable of affecting such financial
interests. Only those financial interests which could be affected
by the filer's official actions must be disclosed, and it is the
water district which has the first and primary responsibility to
identify these potential conflicts of interest and provide for
their disclosure. Hopefully, individuals who object to overly
broad disclosure will take an active role in the design of each
Code's disclosure requirements, so that intrusion of personal
privacy will be the minimum consistent with the objective of
preventing or disclosing conflicts of interest.


F. E. Jarrett
October 19, 1976                                                    Page Two


        The Fair Political Practices Commission is required to and has
provided technical assistance to state and local agencies in draft-
ing Codes. In particular, we have urged agencies to avoid the easy
path of imposing disclosure requirements with a broad brush, but
rather, to describe with as much precision as possible the particu-
lar types of financial interests which could be affected by offi-
cial decisions and which therefore must be disclosed.

        The Political Reform Act was, of course, passed as an initia-
tive. Section 81012(a) of the Act provides that the Act may be
amended to further its purposes. Among those purposes are the
following:

               Assets and income of public officials which may
               be materially affected by their official actions
               should be disclosed and in appropriate circumstances
               the officials should be disqualified from acting in
               order that conflicts of interest may be avoided.

                                    --Government Code Sec. 81002(g)

        Given the circumstances, it is doubtful that the exemption of
water districts' boards of directors from the requirements of the
Act could be construed to be in furtherance to the purposes of
the Act.

        Even assuming, however, that your proposal could be adopted
by the Legislature, and fully recognizing the right of district
directors to express their views, I firmly disagree with the
proposal. Boards of directors of water districts make decisions
of great consequence to their communities. Like other public
officials, board members serve in a position of public trust,
and the people have the right to be assured that their decisions
will be made free from bias caused by their own financial inter-
ests. Disclosure of any financial interests which could foresee-
ably be affected by their official actions is a reasonable and
effective means of preventing conflicts of interest. In recent
years the California Supreme Court and the courts of many other
states have ruled that such disclosure requirement are valid
and that any consequent intrusion of the privacy of the official
is justified by the important public interest that is served.


F. E. Jarrett
October 19, 1976                                                Page Three


     I hope that this information is helpful.  If you have further
questions, please call Mr. Delbert Spurlock, Chief of our Conflicts
of Interest Division, at 916/322-6444.

                                                    Sincerely,


                                                    Michael Bennett
                                                    Executive Director

Burney County Water District
Post Office Box 247, Burney, California 96013 P (916)335-2209

BOARD OF DIRECTORS
Beryl I. Peterson, President
Chester B. Roff, Vice President
Richard A. Casaurang
William E. Shoemaker

SUPERINTENDENT
F. E. Jarrett

SECRETARY
D. Ann Harris

Sep 13 9 11 AM '76

September 9, 1976

State Fair Practice
Box 807
Sacramento, Calif. 95804

Gentlemen:

RE: CONFLICT OF INTEREST

The Board of Directors of the Burney County Water District

at their September 8, 1976, meeting adopted the attached

resolution # 76-20.

They will appreciate any support in regard to this matter.

Very truly yours,

BURNEY COUNTY WATER DISTRICT

F. E. Jarrett
Superintendent

CC: Assemblywoman Pauline Davis
Senator Randolph Collier
Shasta County Board of Supervisors
